name: Deploy

# Manual-only for now (workflow_dispatch): apply-ready scaffolding that never
# fires on its own until the Azure resources exist + secrets/vars are set. Once
# the infra is provisioned (see deploy/README.md), flip on the push trigger:
#   push:
#     branches: [main]
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      image_tag:
        description: "Image tag to build + deploy"
        default: "latest"

concurrency:
  group: deploy-production
  cancel-in-progress: false

# OIDC federated login to Azure (no stored client secret). The federated cred +
# the listed vars/secrets must be configured first — see deploy/README.md.
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

env:
  IMAGE: ${{ vars.ACR_LOGIN_SERVER }}/dataq-backend:${{ inputs.image_tag }}

jobs:
  backend:
    name: Backend — build, migrate, deploy (API + worker)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Build + push backend image to ACR
        run: |
          az acr login --name "${{ vars.ACR_NAME }}"
          docker build -f backend/Dockerfile -t "$IMAGE" .
          docker push "$IMAGE"

      # Migrate BEFORE rolling the app: migrations are additive/backward-compatible
      # (CLAUDE.md), so the running old code tolerates the new schema. Runs the
      # one-off migrate Container Apps Job (provisioned per deploy/README.md).
      # `az containerapp job start` only *starts* the execution and returns, so we
      # capture the execution name and poll it to a terminal state — the rollout
      # below must not begin until the migration has actually SUCCEEDED, and a
      # failed migration must abort the deploy (fail-closed) rather than roll the
      # apps onto an un-migrated schema.
      - name: Run DB migrations (alembic upgrade head) and wait for success
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          exec_name=$(az containerapp job start \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE" \
            --query name -o tsv)
          echo "Started migration execution: $exec_name"
          deadline=$(( SECONDS + 900 ))  # 15-minute cap
          while (( SECONDS < deadline )); do
            status=$(az containerapp job execution list \
              --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
              --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
              --query "[?name=='$exec_name'].properties.status | [0]" -o tsv)
            echo "migration status: ${status:-<pending>}"
            case "$status" in
              Succeeded) echo "Migration succeeded."; exit 0 ;;
              Failed|Degraded|Stopped) echo "::error::Migration $status — aborting before app rollout."; exit 1 ;;
            esac
            sleep 10
          done
          echo "::error::Timed out waiting for migration to complete."; exit 1

      - name: Deploy API
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.API_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

      - name: Deploy worker
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.WORKER_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

  frontend:
    name: Frontend — build + deploy to Static Web App
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml

      - name: Build
        working-directory: frontend
        # VITE_* are inlined at build time. Non-secret azure client/tenant ids
        # come from repo vars; never the SPA secret (there is none — MSAL is public).
        env:
          VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE }}
          VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: "false"
        run: |
          pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
          pnpm build

      - name: Deploy to Azure Static Web Apps
        uses: Azure/static-web-apps-deploy@v1
        with:
          azure_static_web_apps_api_token: ${{ secrets.AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN }}
          action: upload
          app_location: frontend/dist
          skip_app_build: true

# the listed vars/secrets must be configured first — see deploy/README.md.

+  # GHCR registry (ADR 0023) — public package, lowercase owner. ACA pulls it
+  # anonymously, so no registry credential is stored on the apps/job.

      - name: Log in to GHCR

          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build + push backend image to GHCR
        run: |
          docker build -f backend/Dockerfile -t "$IMAGE" .
          docker push "$IMAGE"

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: deploy-production

# OIDC federated login to Azure (no stored client secret). The federated cred +

permissions:

env:

jobs:

      - name: Azure login (OIDC)

          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Build + push backend image to ACR
        run: |
          az acr login --name "${{ vars.ACR_NAME }}"
          docker build -f backend/Dockerfile -t "$IMAGE" .
          docker push "$IMAGE"

      # Migrate BEFORE rolling the app: migrations are additive/backward-compatible
      # (CLAUDE.md), so the running old code tolerates the new schema. Runs the
      # one-off migrate Container Apps Job (provisioned per deploy/README.md).
      # `az containerapp job start` only *starts* the execution and returns, so we
      # capture the execution name and poll it to a terminal state — the rollout
      # below must not begin until the migration has actually SUCCEEDED, and a
      # failed migration must abort the deploy (fail-closed) rather than roll the
      # apps onto an un-migrated schema.
      - name: Run DB migrations (alembic upgrade head) and wait for success
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          exec_name=$(az containerapp job start \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE" \
            --query name -o tsv)
          echo "Started migration execution: $exec_name"
          deadline=$(( SECONDS + 900 ))  # 15-minute cap
          while (( SECONDS < deadline )); do
            status=$(az containerapp job execution list \
              --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
              --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
              --query "[?name=='$exec_name'].properties.status | [0]" -o tsv)
            echo "migration status: ${status:-<pending>}"
            case "$status" in
              Succeeded) echo "Migration succeeded."; exit 0 ;;
              Failed|Degraded|Stopped) echo "::error::Migration $status — aborting before app rollout."; exit 1 ;;
            esac
            sleep 10
          done
          echo "::error::Timed out waiting for migration to complete."; exit 1

      - name: Deploy API
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.API_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

      - name: Deploy worker
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.WORKER_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

  frontend:
    name: Frontend — build + deploy to Static Web App
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4

          node-version: "22"

          cache-dependency-path: frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml

      - name: Build
        working-directory: frontend
        # VITE_* are inlined at build time. Non-secret azure client/tenant ids
        # come from repo vars; never the SPA secret (there is none — MSAL is public).
        env:
          VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE }}
          VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: "false"
        run: |
          pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
          pnpm build

      - name: Deploy to Azure Static Web Apps
        uses: Azure/static-web-apps-deploy@v1
        with:
          azure_static_web_apps_api_token: ${{ secrets.AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN }}
          action: upload
          app_location: frontend/dist
          skip_app_build: true

# DataQ — deployment scaffolding

Apply-ready Azure deploy scaffolding (Week 7). **Nothing here deploys on its own**
— the workflow ([.github/workflows/deploy.yml](../.github/workflows/deploy.yml)) is
`workflow_dispatch`-only until the Azure resources exist and the secrets/vars
below are set. This documents what to provision and how the pieces fit.

Azure is **one** deploy target behind the app's seams (ADR 0010/0013) — the
manifests here are infra config, not business logic. No Azure resource names are
hardcoded in app code; they live only as workflow `vars`/`secrets`.

## Topology

```
Browser ─► Azure Static Web App (frontend/dist)
              │  /api/* proxied to the linked backend (same-origin → no CORS)
              ▼
        Azure Container Apps
          • dataq-api     (FastAPI image, external ingress :8000)
          • dataq-worker  (same image, `celery -A ... worker` + beat)
          • dataq-migrate (Container Apps Job: `alembic upgrade head`)
              │
              ├─► Azure Database for PostgreSQL (DATABASE_URL)
              ├─► Azure Cache for Redis        (REDIS_URL)
              ├─► Azure Key Vault              (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault, managed identity)
              └─► Application Insights         (APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING)
        Azure Container Registry (ACR) — holds the backend image
```

Both api + worker run the **same** image ([backend/Dockerfile](../backend/Dockerfile),
build context = repo root). The frontend is built and uploaded to SWA by the
workflow (the [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile) is the container
alternative — only needed if you run the UI on Container Apps instead of SWA).

## One-time provisioning

The datasource + compute infra is stood up by the external Terraform harness
(ADR 0021) — see `HARNESS_TODO.md`. Beyond that, this app needs:

1. **ACR** + an **ACA environment** + the three apps/job above. The api/worker run
   `uvicorn …` / `celery …`; the migrate **job** runs `alembic upgrade head`.
2. **Managed identity** on the api + worker apps with **Key Vault Secrets User**
   on the vault (so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves `SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`).
3. **App env**: set the keys in [deploy/.env.app.prod.example](.env.app.prod.example)
   on the api + worker apps. Secret values (DB/Redis URL, App Insights) are Key
   Vault-backed Container Apps secrets — never literals.
4. **SWA linked backend**: link the `dataq-api` Container App as the SWA backend so
   `/api/*` is proxied same-origin (then `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` can stay empty). If
   instead the SPA calls the API cross-origin, set `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` to the SWA
   origin (the FastAPI CORS middleware turns on only when it's non-empty).
5. **Azure Monitor → ADF webhook** alert rule (Week-7 task) — needs the deployed
   API URL; configure after the first deploy.

## GitHub config the workflow reads

Set under repo **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions**, and add a
federated credential for OIDC login (subject = this repo's `production`
environment).

**Secrets:** `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID`
(OIDC app registration), `AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN`.

**Variables:** `ACR_NAME`, `ACR_LOGIN_SERVER`, `AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP`,
`API_APP_NAME`, `WORKER_APP_NAME`, `MIGRATE_JOB_NAME`, and the non-secret
`VITE_AZURE_*` build values.

## Going live

1. Provision the resources + set the secrets/vars above.
2. Run the **Deploy** workflow manually (`workflow_dispatch`) to validate end-to-end.
3. To deploy on every merge, uncomment the `push: branches: [main]` trigger in
   the workflow.

Migrations are additive/backward-compatible (CLAUDE.md), so the workflow runs
`alembic upgrade head` **before** rolling the apps — the running old code
tolerates the new schema. (This is exactly the dev-DB step that, when skipped,
500s the checks endpoint after a schema-adding deploy.)

=== backend Dockerfile (entry/cmd) ===
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer. environment.yml's pip section
# does `-r backend/requirements-dev.txt` (which -r's requirements.txt /
# -typecheck / -tooling), so those must exist before `conda env create`. Copy
# just the requirements files (not all of backend/) so the env-create layer cache
# busts only on a pin change, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy

ENV PATH=/opt/conda/envs/dataq/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is needed to run pytest/mypy in-container).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker service overrides `command` in compose.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

input.content:
# Key Vault — the app's runtime SecretStore (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault). It
# holds the datasource connection credentials the app writes/reads via the API at
# runtime, plus the pre-seeded orchestration webhook secrets. RBAC authorization
# (not access policies): the UAMI gets Secrets User (read); the deployer gets
# Secrets Officer (write) so Terraform can seed the webhook secrets.
#
# NOTE: boot-critical config (DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL / App Insights) is injected
# as inline Container App secrets in containerapps.tf, NOT via KV references — that
# decouples first-revision activation from KV-RBAC propagation delay (the classic
# "secret ref fails on the very first apply" gotcha). The vault is still exercised
# end-to-end by the UAMI read path + the webhook secrets below.

resource "azurerm_key_vault" "app" {
  name                      = "dataq-app-kv-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  location                  = var.azure_location
  resource_group_name       = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  tenant_id                 = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  sku_name                  = "standard"
  enable_rbac_authorization = true
  purge_protection_enabled  = false
  tags                      = local.common_tags

  depends_on = [azurerm_resource_provider_registration.keyvault]
}

# Deployer (Owner user running this apply) -> write secrets.
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_deployer" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets Officer"
  principal_id         = data.azurerm_client_config.current.object_id
}

# App identity -> read secrets at runtime (DefaultAzureCredential).
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_app_reader" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets User"
  principal_id         = azurerm_user_assigned_identity.app.principal_id
}

# RBAC data-plane role assignments are eventually consistent — wait before the
# first secret write so Terraform doesn't 403 immediately after the grant.
resource "time_sleep" "kv_rbac_propagation" {
  create_duration = "60s"
  depends_on      = [azurerm_role_assignment.kv_deployer]
}

# ── Pre-seeded webhook secrets (orchestration event auth) ────────────────────
# Names match deploy/.env.app.prod.example (ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME etc.). Values
# are generated; rotate via the provider rotation path (ADR 0006/0007).

resource "random_password" "adf_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "random_password" "airflow_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "adf_webhook" {
  name         = "adf-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.adf_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "airflow_webhook" {
  name         = "airflow-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.airflow_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

input.content:
# App database — Postgres Flexible Server (Burstable B1ms). Public access +
# "allow Azure services" so the Container Apps environment reaches it without VNet
# integration (single-tenant v1). Password generated locally — never committed;
# state is gitignored. westus3 (see postgres_location) avoids the wus2 offer
# restriction the harness also hit.

resource "random_password" "pg" {
  length  = 28
  special = false # keep the SQLAlchemy URL clean (no %-encoding)
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "app" {
  name                          = "dataq-app-pg-wus3-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  resource_group_name           = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location                      = var.postgres_location
  version                       = "16"
  administrator_login           = var.postgres_admin_login
  administrator_password        = random_password.pg.result
  sku_name                      = var.postgres_sku
  storage_mb                    = 32768
  auto_grow_enabled             = true
  public_network_access_enabled = true
  zone                          = "1"
  tags                          = local.common_tags
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "app" {
  name      = "dataq"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  collation = "en_US.utf8"
  charset   = "utf8"
}

# start=end=0.0.0.0 is Azure's special "allow access from Azure services" rule.
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "azure_services" {
  name             = "allow-azure-services"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  start_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
  end_ip_address   = "0.0.0.0"
}

locals {
  # psycopg2 URL the backend Settings read as DATABASE_URL. sslmode=require —
  # Flexible Server enforces TLS.
  database_url = join("", [
    "postgresql+psycopg2://",
    var.postgres_admin_login, ":", random_password.pg.result,
    "@", azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.fqdn, ":5432/",
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database.app.name, "?sslmode=require",
  ])
}

input.content:
# Static Web App — hosts the React/Vite build. Standard SKU so the api Container
# App can be linked as a backend: SWA then proxies /api/* to it same-origin, which
# is exactly what frontend/staticwebapp.config.json + the relative axios baseURL
# ('/api/v1') expect (no CORS). The SPA content is uploaded by the Deploy workflow
# (Azure/static-web-apps-deploy) using the api token output below.

resource "azurerm_static_web_app" "app" {
  name                = "dataq-app-web"
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location            = var.azure_location
  sku_tier            = var.swa_sku
  sku_size            = var.swa_sku
  tags                = local.common_tags

  depends_on = [azurerm_resource_provider_registration.web]
}

# Link the api Container App as the SWA backend. No azurerm resource covers
# arbitrary linked backends (only the Functions registration), so use the CLI.
# `backends link` errors if a backend is already linked, so tolerate that on
# re-apply; re-run after changing the api by tainting this resource.
resource "null_resource" "swa_linked_backend" {
  triggers = {
    swa_id = azurerm_static_web_app.app.id
    api_id = azurerm_container_app.api.id
  }
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = <<-CMD
      az staticwebapp backends link \
        --name ${azurerm_static_web_app.app.name} \
        --resource-group ${data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name} \
        --backend-resource-id ${azurerm_container_app.api.id} \
        --backend-region ${var.azure_location} \
        --only-show-errors --output none || echo "backend already linked (or link pending) — continuing"
    CMD
  }
  depends_on = [azurerm_static_web_app.app, azurerm_container_app.api]
}

toolUseResult.content:
# Static Web App — hosts the React/Vite build. Standard SKU so the api Container
# App can be linked as a backend: SWA then proxies /api/* to it same-origin, which
# is exactly what frontend/staticwebapp.config.json + the relative axios baseURL
# ('/api/v1') expect (no CORS). The SPA content is uploaded by the Deploy workflow
# (Azure/static-web-apps-deploy) using the api token output below.

resource "azurerm_static_web_app" "app" {
  name                = "dataq-app-web"
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location            = var.azure_location
  sku_tier            = var.swa_sku
  sku_size            = var.swa_sku
  tags                = local.common_tags

  depends_on = [azurerm_resource_provider_registration.web]
}

# Link the api Container App as the SWA backend. No azurerm resource covers
# arbitrary linked backends (only the Functions registration), so use the CLI.
# `backends link` errors if a backend is already linked, so tolerate that on
# re-apply; re-run after changing the api by tainting this resource.
resource "null_resource" "swa_linked_backend" {
  triggers = {
    swa_id = azurerm_static_web_app.app.id
    api_id = azurerm_container_app.api.id
  }
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = <<-CMD
      az staticwebapp backends link \
        --name ${azurerm_static_web_app.app.name} \
        --resource-group ${data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name} \
        --backend-resource-id ${azurerm_container_app.api.id} \
        --backend-region ${var.azure_location} \
        --only-show-errors --output none || echo "backend already linked (or link pending) — continuing"
    CMD
  }
  depends_on = [azurerm_static_web_app.app, azurerm_container_app.api]
}

content.text: Now the terraform README, then validate the whole stack:

input.content:
# DataQ — app infra (Terraform)

Provisions the DataQ **application's own** production resources into the existing
`dataq-rg`. Deliberately **separate** from the harness stack
(`~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/terraform`, ADR 0021) — only the subscription +
RG are shared. All resources here are prefixed `dataq-app-*` and tagged
`purpose=dataq-app`; the harness's `dataq-harness-*` / `purpose=dataq-harness`
resources are never touched.

## What it creates

| Resource | Name |
|---|---|
| Log Analytics workspace | `dataq-app-logs` |
| Application Insights | `dataq-app-ai` |
| User-assigned identity | `dataq-app-id` (api/worker → Key Vault) |
| Key Vault (RBAC) | `dataq-app-kv-<suffix>` (SecretStore + webhook secrets) |
| Postgres Flexible Server | `dataq-app-pg-wus3-<suffix>` (westus3, B1ms) |
| Container Apps environment | `dataq-app-cae` |
| Redis broker (Container App) | `dataq-app-redis` (internal TCP) |
| API / worker / migrate | `dataq-api` · `dataq-worker` · `dataq-migrate` (job) |
| Static Web App (Standard) | `dataq-app-web` (+ linked api backend) |
| GitHub-deploy app registration | `dataq-github-deploy` (OIDC federated cred) |

Backend image: `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:<image_tag>` (GHCR, public —
ACA pulls anonymously, ADR 0023). It must exist + be **public** before apply.

## Prerequisites

- `az login` as a subscription **Owner** (this stack registers RPs + creates role
  assignments + an AAD app registration).
- The GHCR backend image pushed + public (see repo root `deploy/README.md` /
  the Week-7 bring-up steps).
- State is **local + gitignored** — do not commit `terraform.tfstate`.

## Apply

```bash
cd deploy/terraform
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars   # optional; defaults work
terraform init
terraform plan      # review
terraform apply
```

## After apply — wire the Deploy workflow

`.github/workflows/deploy.yml` reads these. Push them with `gh` from the outputs:

```bash
gh secret  set AZURE_CLIENT_ID       -b "$(terraform output -raw github_actions_client_id)"
gh secret  set AZURE_TENANT_ID       -b "$(terraform output -raw azure_tenant_id)"
gh secret  set AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID -b "$(terraform output -raw azure_subscription_id)"
gh secret  set AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN -b "$(terraform output -raw swa_api_token)"
gh variable set AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP -b "$(terraform output -raw resource_group)"
gh variable set API_APP_NAME         -b "$(terraform output -raw api_app_name)"
gh variable set WORKER_APP_NAME      -b "$(terraform output -raw worker_app_name)"
gh variable set MIGRATE_JOB_NAME     -b "$(terraform output -raw migrate_job_name)"
# Plus the non-secret VITE_AZURE_* build vars (tenant/spa/api client ids + scope).
```

Create the `production` GitHub environment (the federated credential subject is
`repo:<owner>/<repo>:environment:production`).

## Verify

```bash
# Exactly the app stack — harness untouched:
az resource list -g dataq-rg --query "[?tags.purpose=='dataq-app'].name" -o tsv

# Re-plan is clean (idempotent):
terraform plan      # expect: No changes

# API health + Swagger:
curl -fsS "$(terraform output -raw api_url)/docs" >/dev/null && echo OK
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Key Vault — the app's runtime SecretStore (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault). It
# holds the datasource connection credentials the app writes/reads via the API at
# runtime, plus the pre-seeded orchestration webhook secrets. RBAC authorization
# (not access policies): the UAMI gets Secrets User (read); the deployer gets
# Secrets Officer (write) so Terraform can seed the webhook secrets.
#
# NOTE: boot-critical config (DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL / App Insights) is injected
# as inline Container App secrets in containerapps.tf, NOT via KV references — that
# decouples first-revision activation from KV-RBAC propagation delay (the classic
# "secret ref fails on the very first apply" gotcha). The vault is still exercised
# end-to-end by the UAMI read path + the webhook secrets below.

resource "azurerm_key_vault" "app" {
  name                      = "dataq-app-kv-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  location                  = var.azure_location
  resource_group_name       = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  tenant_id                 = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  sku_name                  = "standard"
  enable_rbac_authorization = true
  purge_protection_enabled  = false
  tags                      = local.common_tags

  depends_on = [azurerm_resource_provider_registration.keyvault]
}

# Deployer (Owner user running this apply) -> write secrets.
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_deployer" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets Officer"
  principal_id         = data.azurerm_client_config.current.object_id
}

# App identity -> read secrets at runtime (DefaultAzureCredential).
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_app_reader" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets User"
  principal_id         = azurerm_user_assigned_identity.app.principal_id
}

# RBAC data-plane role assignments are eventually consistent — wait before the
# first secret write so Terraform doesn't 403 immediately after the grant.
resource "time_sleep" "kv_rbac_propagation" {
  create_duration = "60s"
  depends_on      = [azurerm_role_assignment.kv_deployer]
}

# ── Pre-seeded webhook secrets (orchestration event auth) ────────────────────
# Names match deploy/.env.app.prod.example (ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME etc.). Values
# are generated; rotate via the provider rotation path (ADR 0006/0007).

resource "random_password" "adf_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "random_password" "airflow_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "adf_webhook" {
  name         = "adf-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.adf_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "airflow_webhook" {
  name         = "airflow-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.airflow_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

  anonymously (public package, ADR 0023 — no registry block).

- GitHub OIDC: dataq-github-deploy app registration + federated credential

- App Insights, Log Analytics, RP registration for the 4 NotRegistered providers.

content.text:
The `write:packages` scope is now granted — I'm unblocked on the GHCR push.

On your **Slack question** first:

For DataQ's alerting (which today is the `ResultPublisher` → Teams adaptive-card path with a webhook secret in Key Vault), adding Slack is a near-mirror. What's needed depends on which mechanism:

- **Slack Incoming Webhook (recommended, matches the Teams pattern):** just **one webhook URL** — `https://hooks.slack.com/services/T…/B…/…`. You create a Slack app, enable *Incoming Webhooks*, and add it to a channel; the channel is bound into the URL. We'd store it as a Key Vault secret (e.g. `SLACK_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME`, parallel to `TEAMS_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME`) and POST Block Kit JSON. **No bot token, no channel ID required.**
- **Slack Web API (`chat.postMessage`)** — only if you need threading, message edits, or @-mentions by user ID: a **bot token** (`xoxb-…`), the **`chat:write`** scope, and the **channel ID**.

So for the simplest "fire an alert into a channel," all you need is the **incoming webhook URL**. Tell me which mechanism and whether it's workspace-wide or per-suite (like the #373/#378 per-suite notification config) and I can scope it as a new `ResultPublisher` channel.

Meanwhile I'll keep the cloud deploy moving — logging Docker into GHCR and kicking off the backend image build (it's the long pole, so I'll run it in the background):

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Key Vault — the app's runtime SecretStore (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault). It
# holds the datasource connection credentials the app writes/reads via the API at
# runtime, plus the pre-seeded orchestration webhook secrets. RBAC authorization
# (not access policies): the UAMI gets Secrets User (read); the deployer gets
# Secrets Officer (write) so Terraform can seed the webhook secrets.
#
# NOTE: boot-critical config (DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL / App Insights) is injected
# as inline Container App secrets in containerapps.tf, NOT via KV references — that
# decouples first-revision activation from KV-RBAC propagation delay (the classic
# "secret ref fails on the very first apply" gotcha). The vault is still exercised
# end-to-end by the UAMI read path + the webhook secrets below.

resource "azurerm_key_vault" "app" {
  name                       = "dataq-app-kv-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  location                   = var.azure_location
  resource_group_name        = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  tenant_id                  = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  sku_name                   = "standard"
  rbac_authorization_enabled = true
  purge_protection_enabled   = false
  tags                       = local.common_tags

  depends_on = [azurerm_resource_provider_registration.keyvault]
}

# Deployer (Owner user running this apply) -> write secrets.
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_deployer" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets Officer"
  principal_id         = data.azurerm_client_config.current.object_id
}

# App identity -> read secrets at runtime (DefaultAzureCredential).
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "kv_app_reader" {
  scope                = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets User"
  principal_id         = azurerm_user_assigned_identity.app.principal_id
}

# RBAC data-plane role assignments are eventually consistent — wait before the
# first secret write so Terraform doesn't 403 immediately after the grant.
resource "time_sleep" "kv_rbac_propagation" {
  create_duration = "60s"
  depends_on      = [azurerm_role_assignment.kv_deployer]
}

# ── Pre-seeded webhook secrets (orchestration event auth) ────────────────────
# Names match deploy/.env.app.prod.example (ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME etc.). Values
# are generated; rotate via the provider rotation path (ADR 0006/0007).

resource "random_password" "adf_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "random_password" "airflow_webhook" {
  length  = 40
  special = false
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "adf_webhook" {
  name         = "adf-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.adf_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "airflow_webhook" {
  name         = "airflow-webhook-secret"
  value        = random_password.airflow_webhook.result
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.app.id
  depends_on   = [time_sleep.kv_rbac_propagation]
}

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# App database — Postgres Flexible Server (Burstable B1ms). Public access +
# "allow Azure services" so the Container Apps environment reaches it without VNet
# integration (single-tenant v1). Password generated locally — never committed;
# state is gitignored. westus3 (see postgres_location) avoids the wus2 offer
# restriction the harness also hit.

resource "random_password" "pg" {
  length  = 28
  special = false # keep the SQLAlchemy URL clean (no %-encoding)
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "app" {
  name                          = "dataq-app-pg-wus3-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  resource_group_name           = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location                      = var.postgres_location
  version                       = "16"
  administrator_login           = var.postgres_admin_login
  administrator_password        = random_password.pg.result
  sku_name                      = var.postgres_sku
  storage_mb                    = 32768
  auto_grow_enabled             = true
  public_network_access_enabled = true
  zone                          = "1"
  tags                          = local.common_tags
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "app" {
  name      = "dataq"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  collation = "en_US.utf8"
  charset   = "utf8"
}

# start=end=0.0.0.0 is Azure's special "allow access from Azure services" rule.
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "azure_services" {
  name             = "allow-azure-services"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  start_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
  end_ip_address   = "0.0.0.0"
}

locals {
  # psycopg2 URL the backend Settings read as DATABASE_URL. sslmode=require —
  # Flexible Server enforces TLS.
  database_url = join("", [
    "postgresql+psycopg2://",
    var.postgres_admin_login, ":", random_password.pg.result,
    "@", azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.fqdn, ":5432/",
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database.app.name, "?sslmode=require",
  ])
}

content.text: Make the allow-Azure-services firewall rule conditional on public access (so the hardened path stays consistent):

input.old_string:
# start=end=0.0.0.0 is Azure's special "allow access from Azure services" rule.
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "azure_services" {
  name             = "allow-azure-services"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  start_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
  end_ip_address   = "0.0.0.0"
}
input.new_string:
# start=end=0.0.0.0 is Azure's special "allow access from Azure services" rule —
# only meaningful while public access is on. Disappears under the VNet-private
# hardening path (var.postgres_public_network_access=false).
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "azure_services" {
  count            = var.postgres_public_network_access ? 1 : 0
  name             = "allow-azure-services"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.app.id
  start_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
  end_ip_address   = "0.0.0.0"
}

content.text: Build is running in the background. While it builds (conda env under amd64 emulation takes a while), let me commit the security hardening and verify the build is progressing:

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ; git add deploy/terraform/ && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(deploy): harden terraform per security review (least-priv, redis auth)

Address the 4 commit-security-review findings on the app infra stack:

- CI principal: scope Contributor to the 3 deploy targets (api/worker/migrate
  job) instead of the whole dataq-rg — the RG is SHARED with the harness, so an
  RG-wide grant let the CI SP modify harness resources. (least privilege)
- Redis broker: add --requirepass with a generated password injected via a
  Container App secret; REDIS_URL now carries the password and is injected as a
  secret (was plain env). Defense-in-depth atop the internal-only ingress.
- Postgres public access -> var.postgres_public_network_access (default true for
  v1; flip false for the VNet-private hardening path). allow-azure-services
  firewall rule is now conditional on it.
- Key Vault purge protection -> var.key_vault_purge_protection (false during
  bring-up for name reuse; set true for prod). Documented as a prod toggle.

content.content:
1	# ADR 0023 — Container image registry: GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) over ACR / Docker Hub
2	
3	- **Status:** Accepted
4	- **Date:** 2026-06-27
5	- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
6	- **Related:** ADR [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (Azure is one impl behind
7	  each seam — the registry is infra, not business logic), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md)
8	  (BYOL/marketplace distribution — a neutral registry doubles as the future distribution channel),
9	  [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) (deploy/harness context). Supersedes the ACR
10	  choice scaffolded in the Week-7 deploy work (#379); rides the `deploy.yml` SHA-pin (#382).
11	
12	## Context
13	
14	The Week-7 deploy scaffolding (#379) wired `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` to **Azure Container
15	Registry (ACR)**: backend image built in CI, pushed to ACR, pulled by Azure Container Apps (api +
16	worker share one image; a migrate Job runs `alembic upgrade head`). Before the apply, we reconsidered
17	the registry. The DataQ repo is **public**, and a standing project principle (ADR 0010/0013) is that
18	**Azure is one implementation behind each seam** — no Azure-specific assumption should be load-bearing
19	where a neutral option is equivalent. A registry is pure infra; ACR couples the artifact path to Azure
20	for no functional gain.
21	
22	Options weighed: **ACR**, **Docker Hub**, **GHCR**.
23	
24	## Decision
25	
26	**Use GitHub Container Registry (`ghcr.io`) as the container registry for the backend image.**
27	
28	- CI pushes `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${{ github.sha }}` (immutable SHA tag, #382),
29	  authenticating with the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`permissions: packages: write`) — **no separate
30	  registry account or stored PAT** in CI.
31	- The package is published **public** (the repo is already public and the image bakes in no secrets —
32	  credentials are injected at runtime via Key Vault / Container Apps secrets per the working
33	  agreement), so **Azure Container Apps pulls anonymously — no registry credential stored on the
34	  apps**. This preserves the "no literals / no long-lived registry secret" posture that ACR's managed
35	  identity gave us, without the Azure coupling.
36	- The migrate Job, api, and worker all pull the **same** SHA-pinned image.
37	
38	## Consequences
39	
40	**Positive**
41	- **Vendor-neutral** — the artifact path no longer assumes Azure (ADR 0010/0013). If DataQ moves off
42	  Azure, the registry doesn't move with it.
43	- **Free** for public images; **no Docker Hub pull-rate throttling** on our own GHCR images (matters
44	  under ACA autoscaling + worker + migrate Job all pulling).
45	- **No extra credential** — CI uses `GITHUB_TOKEN`; ACA pulls a public package anonymously. One fewer
46	  secret to rotate than Docker Hub (PAT) and parity with ACR's MI on the "no literals" goal.
47	- **Doubles as the BYOL/marketplace distribution registry** (ADR 0013) — a neutral public registry is
48	  the natural home for a customer-pullable image post-v1.
49	- Same GitHub surface as the source + Actions — one fewer system to provision/manage than ACR.
50	
51	**Negative / watch**
52	- Loses ACR's in-region **co-location** with ACA → pulls cross the internet (added cold-start/scale-out
53	  latency) and loses **Defender for Cloud** registry vulnerability scanning inside the Azure trust
54	  boundary. Mitigation: image scanning can run in CI (e.g. Trivy/Scout) independent of the registry;
55	  the security cadence (CLAUDE.md §6) already covers SAST/deps.
56	- If the image ever needs to be **private** (e.g. a closed BYOL build), GHCR pull from ACA requires a
57	  GitHub PAT as a Container Apps registry secret — re-introducing a stored credential. Revisit then.
58	- Relies on GHCR availability for deploys/scale-out (as any external registry would).
59	
60	## Alternatives considered
61	
62	- **Azure Container Registry (ACR).** Best purely *in-Azure*: managed-identity pull (no secret),
63	  region co-location, Defender scanning. Rejected as the default because it couples the artifact path
64	  to Azure (against ADR 0010/0013) and carries a small standing cost, for benefits that are latency/
65	  scanning niceties rather than correctness. Remains a valid swap if a fully-in-Azure trust boundary is
66	  later required — the registry is config, not code.
67	- **Docker Hub.** Vendor-neutral and free for public images, but **pull-rate limits** (anon/free tiers)
68	  are a real risk under ACA scale-out, and it needs a **separate account + PAT** stored as an ACA
69	  registry credential. Strictly worse than GHCR here (GHCR has neither problem and reuses GitHub auth).
70	  Better reserved as an optional *additional* public distribution mirror if ever wanted.
71

file.content:
# ADR 0023 — Container image registry: GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) over ACR / Docker Hub

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-27
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
- **Related:** ADR [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (Azure is one impl behind
  each seam — the registry is infra, not business logic), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md)
  (BYOL/marketplace distribution — a neutral registry doubles as the future distribution channel),
  [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) (deploy/harness context). Supersedes the ACR
  choice scaffolded in the Week-7 deploy work (#379); rides the `deploy.yml` SHA-pin (#382).

## Context

The Week-7 deploy scaffolding (#379) wired `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` to **Azure Container
Registry (ACR)**: backend image built in CI, pushed to ACR, pulled by Azure Container Apps (api +
worker share one image; a migrate Job runs `alembic upgrade head`). Before the apply, we reconsidered
the registry. The DataQ repo is **public**, and a standing project principle (ADR 0010/0013) is that
**Azure is one implementation behind each seam** — no Azure-specific assumption should be load-bearing
where a neutral option is equivalent. A registry is pure infra; ACR couples the artifact path to Azure
for no functional gain.

Options weighed: **ACR**, **Docker Hub**, **GHCR**.

## Decision

**Use GitHub Container Registry (`ghcr.io`) as the container registry for the backend image.**

- CI pushes `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${{ github.sha }}` (immutable SHA tag, #382),
  authenticating with the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`permissions: packages: write`) — **no separate
  registry account or stored PAT** in CI.
- The package is published **public** (the repo is already public and the image bakes in no secrets —
  credentials are injected at runtime via Key Vault / Container Apps secrets per the working
  agreement), so **Azure Container Apps pulls anonymously — no registry credential stored on the
  apps**. This preserves the "no literals / no long-lived registry secret" posture that ACR's managed
  identity gave us, without the Azure coupling.
- The migrate Job, api, and worker all pull the **same** SHA-pinned image.

## Consequences

**Positive**
- **Vendor-neutral** — the artifact path no longer assumes Azure (ADR 0010/0013). If DataQ moves off
  Azure, the registry doesn't move with it.
- **Free** for public images; **no Docker Hub pull-rate throttling** on our own GHCR images (matters
  under ACA autoscaling + worker + migrate Job all pulling).
- **No extra credential** — CI uses `GITHUB_TOKEN`; ACA pulls a public package anonymously. One fewer
  secret to rotate than Docker Hub (PAT) and parity with ACR's MI on the "no literals" goal.
- **Doubles as the BYOL/marketplace distribution registry** (ADR 0013) — a neutral public registry is
  the natural home for a customer-pullable image post-v1.
- Same GitHub surface as the source + Actions — one fewer system to provision/manage than ACR.

**Negative / watch**
- Loses ACR's in-region **co-location** with ACA → pulls cross the internet (added cold-start/scale-out
  latency) and loses **Defender for Cloud** registry vulnerability scanning inside the Azure trust
  boundary. Mitigation: image scanning can run in CI (e.g. Trivy/Scout) independent of the registry;
  the security cadence (CLAUDE.md §6) already covers SAST/deps.
- If the image ever needs to be **private** (e.g. a closed BYOL build), GHCR pull from ACA requires a
  GitHub PAT as a Container Apps registry secret — re-introducing a stored credential. Revisit then.
- Relies on GHCR availability for deploys/scale-out (as any external registry would).

## Alternatives considered

- **Azure Container Registry (ACR).** Best purely *in-Azure*: managed-identity pull (no secret),
  region co-location, Defender scanning. Rejected as the default because it couples the artifact path
  to Azure (against ADR 0010/0013) and carries a small standing cost, for benefits that are latency/
  scanning niceties rather than correctness. Remains a valid swap if a fully-in-Azure trust boundary is
  later required — the registry is config, not code.
- **Docker Hub.** Vendor-neutral and free for public images, but **pull-rate limits** (anon/free tiers)
  are a real risk under ACA scale-out, and it needs a **separate account + PAT** stored as an ACA
  registry credential. Strictly worse than GHCR here (GHCR has neither problem and reuses GitHub auth).
  Better reserved as an optional *additional* public distribution mirror if ever wanted.

content.text: ADR format is clear. Let me write ADR 0024 while the apply continues:

input.content:
# ADR 0024 — App deployment infrastructure: Terraform (ACA + SWA + KV + self-hosted Redis), separate from the harness

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-27
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
- **Related:** ADR [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (Azure = one impl behind each
  seam), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md) (BYOL distribution), [0018](0018-results-surface-and-grafana-deferral.md)
  (in-app surface → same-origin SPA), [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) (the harness
  infra is out-of-repo and separate), [0023](0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) (GHCR registry).
  Realizes the Week-7 deploy and supersedes the #379 scaffolding's ACR assumption.

## Context

Week 7 is "go to cloud." The #379 scaffolding documented a topology but had **no infra-as-code** for the
app's own resources, and still assumed ACR (superseded by ADR 0023). Separately, the demo/datasource
infra already lives in an **out-of-repo** Terraform harness (ADR 0021) in the shared `dataq-rg`. We had
to decide: where the app's production IaC lives, how it stays isolated from the harness, and the
concrete service choices (registry pull, Redis, secrets, frontend↔API wiring, CI auth).

## Decision

**Provision the app's production resources with a dedicated, in-repo Terraform stack at
`deploy/terraform/`, reusing only the subscription + `dataq-rg`, and isolated from the harness.**

- **In-repo IaC, local gitignored state.** Unlike the harness (out-of-repo, ADR 0021), this is the
  *app's own* deploy config and belongs with the code; state is gitignored (it holds the generated
  Postgres/Redis passwords). The provider lock is tracked.
- **Strict separation in the shared RG.** All resources are `dataq-app-*` and tagged
  `purpose=dataq-app` (harness = `dataq-harness-*` / `purpose=dataq-harness`). The RG is **referenced,
  never managed** — an **idempotent `az group create` step** (preserving the existing `project=dataq`
  tag) guarantees it exists without Terraform owning/destroying the shared RG.
- **Compute = Azure Container Apps.** `dataq-api` (external ingress), `dataq-worker` (Celery + embedded
  beat), `dataq-migrate` (manual Job: `alembic upgrade head`). All run the **same GHCR image** (ADR
  0023), pulled **anonymously** (public package — no registry block/credential).
- **Redis = self-hosted `redis:7-alpine` Container App** (internal TCP), not Azure Cache for Redis. The
  Celery broker is transient (no persistence SLA needed); self-hosting is materially cheaper for v1. It
  still requires a password (`--requirepass`) as defense-in-depth atop internal-only ingress.
- **Secrets.** Key Vault (RBAC) is the app's runtime `SecretStore` (`SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`),
  read via a **user-assigned managed identity** (Key Vault Secrets User). Boot-critical config
  (`DATABASE_URL`, `REDIS_URL`, App Insights) is injected as **inline Container App secrets**, *not* KV
  references — decoupling first-revision activation from KV-RBAC propagation delay. Webhook secrets are
  pre-seeded in KV.
- **Frontend ↔ API = SWA Standard + linked backend.** The SPA uses a relative `/api/v1` base URL, so
  the Static Web App links the `dataq-api` Container App as its backend and proxies `/api/*`
  **same-origin** (CORS middleware stays off — consistent with the existing `staticwebapp.config.json`
  and ADR 0018's same-origin authz). Linking uses `az staticwebapp backends link` (no azurerm resource
  covers arbitrary linked backends).
- **CI auth = AAD app registration + GitHub OIDC.** A `dataq-github-deploy` app registration with a
  federated credential (`repo:…:environment:production`) — no stored client secret. Its Contributor
  role is **scoped to the three deploy targets**, not the shared RG (least privilege; the RG holds
  harness resources).
- **Hardening toggles.** `postgres_public_network_access` (true for v1 over the allow-Azure-services
  firewall + TLS; flip to false for the VNet-private path) and `key_vault_purge_protection` (false for
  bring-up name reuse; true for prod) are explicit variables, not silent defaults.

## Consequences

**Positive**
- App IaC is reviewable, reproducible, and idempotent (`plan` is clean on re-run); the shared RG and
  harness resources are provably untouched (`az resource list … purpose=='dataq-app'`).
- No Azure registry, no stored registry/CI secret (GHCR public + OIDC); cheapest viable Redis.
- Same-origin SPA↔API needs no CORS and matches the committed SWA config.
- Least-privilege CI principal can't reach harness resources in the shared RG.

**Negative / watch**
- **Self-hosted Redis** has no managed HA/persistence; a replica restart drops in-flight broker state
  (acceptable for a Celery broker — tasks re-dispatch). Revisit Azure Cache if durability is needed.
- **Postgres public access** (v1) is gated only by the allow-Azure-services rule + TLS + credentials;
  the VNet-private path is the post-v1 hardening (variable already in place).
- **Linked-backend via CLI** (`null_resource`) is outside Terraform's graph — re-link by tainting it if
  the API resource id changes.
- Cross-internet GHCR pulls add cold-start latency (already noted in ADR 0023).

## Alternatives considered

- **Out-of-repo, harness-style (ADR 0021 precedent).** Rejected: that precedent is for *demo/datasource*
  infra; the app's deploy config belongs with the app (state still gitignored, no secrets in files).
- **Azure Cache for Redis (managed).** Rejected for v1 on cost; the broker doesn't need a managed SLA.
- **SWA Free + CORS (absolute API URL).** Rejected: needs a frontend code change (relative `/api/v1`
  baseURL) and re-introduces CORS; linked backend is the same-origin design already committed.
- **User-assigned identity + federated cred for CI** (instead of an app registration). Viable and needs
  no app-reg rights; the app registration was chosen for a cleaner separation of the CI principal.

  seam), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md) (BYOL distribution), [0018](0018-results-surface-and-grafana-deferral.md)
  (in-app surface → same-origin SPA), [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) (the harness
  infra is out-of-repo and separate), [0023](0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) (GHCR registry).
  Realizes the Week-7 deploy and supersedes the #379 scaffolding's ACR assumption.

## Context

Week 7 is "go to cloud." The #379 scaffolding documented a topology but had **no infra-as-code** for the
app's own resources, and still assumed ACR (superseded by ADR 0023). Separately, the demo/datasource
infra already lives in an **out-of-repo** Terraform harness (ADR 0021) in the shared `dataq-rg`. We had
to decide: where the app's production IaC lives, how it stays isolated from the harness, and the
concrete service choices (registry pull, Redis, secrets, frontend↔API wiring, CI auth).

## Decision

**Provision the app's production resources with a dedicated, in-repo Terraform stack at
`deploy/terraform/`, reusing only the subscription + `dataq-rg`, and isolated from the harness.**

- **In-repo IaC, local gitignored state.** Unlike the harness (out-of-repo, ADR 0021), this is the
  *app's own* deploy config and belongs with the code; state is gitignored (it holds the generated
  Postgres/Redis passwords). The provider lock is tracked.
- **Strict separation in the shared RG.** All resources are `dataq-app-*` and tagged
  `purpose=dataq-app` (harness = `dataq-harness-*` / `purpose=dataq-harness`). The RG is **referenced,
  never managed** — an **idempotent `az group create` step** (preserving the existing `project=dataq`
  tag) guarantees it exists without Terraform owning/destroying the shared RG.
- **Compute = Azure Container Apps.** `dataq-api` (external ingress), `dataq-worker` (Celery + embedded
  beat), `dataq-migrate` (manual Job: `alembic upgrade head`). All run the **same GHCR image** (ADR
  0023), pulled **anonymously** (public package — no registry block/credential).
- **Redis = self-hosted `redis:7-alpine` Container App** (internal TCP), not Azure Cache for Redis. The
  Celery broker is transient (no persistence SLA needed); self-hosting is materially cheaper for v1. It
  still requires a password (`--requirepass`) as defense-in-depth atop internal-only ingress.
- **Secrets.** Key Vault (RBAC) is the app's runtime `SecretStore` (`SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`),
  read via a **user-assigned managed identity** (Key Vault Secrets User). Boot-critical config
  (`DATABASE_URL`, `REDIS_URL`, App Insights) is injected as **inline Container App secrets**, *not* KV
  references — decoupling first-revision activation from KV-RBAC propagation delay. Webhook secrets are
  pre-seeded in KV.
- **Frontend ↔ API = SWA Standard + linked backend.** The SPA uses a relative `/api/v1` base URL, so
  the Static Web App links the `dataq-api` Container App as its backend and proxies `/api/*`
  **same-origin** (CORS middleware stays off — consistent with the existing `staticwebapp.config.json`
  and ADR 0018's same-origin authz). Linking uses `az staticwebapp backends link` (no azurerm resource
  covers arbitrary linked backends).
- **CI auth = AAD app registration + GitHub OIDC.** A `dataq-github-deploy` app registration with a
  federated credential (`repo:…:environment:production`) — no stored client secret. Its Contributor
  role is **scoped to the three deploy targets**, not the shared RG (least privilege; the RG holds
  harness resources).
- **Hardening toggles.** `postgres_public_network_access` (true for v1 over the allow-Azure-services
  firewall + TLS; flip to false for the VNet-private path) and `key_vault_purge_protection` (false for
  bring-up name reuse; true for prod) are explicit variables, not silent defaults.

## Consequences

**Positive**
- App IaC is reviewable, reproducible, and idempotent (`plan` is clean on re-run); the shared RG and
  harness resources are provably untouched (`az resource list … purpose=='dataq-app'`).
- No Azure registry, no stored registry/CI secret (GHCR public + OIDC); cheapest viable Redis.
- Same-origin SPA↔API needs no CORS and matches the committed SWA config.
- Least-privilege CI principal can't reach harness resources in the shared RG.

**Negative / watch**
- **Self-hosted Redis** has no managed HA/persistence; a replica restart drops in-flight broker state
  (acceptable for a Celery broker — tasks re-dispatch). Revisit Azure Cache if durability is needed.
- **Postgres public access** (v1) is gated only by the allow-Azure-services rule + TLS + credentials;
  the VNet-private path is the post-v1 hardening (variable already in place).
- **Linked-backend via CLI** (`null_resource`) is outside Terraform's graph — re-link by tainting it if
  the API resource id changes.
- Cross-internet GHCR pulls add cold-start latency (already noted in ADR 0023).

## Alternatives considered

- **Out-of-repo, harness-style (ADR 0021 precedent).** Rejected: that precedent is for *demo/datasource*
  infra; the app's deploy config belongs with the app (state still gitignored, no secrets in files).
- **Azure Cache for Redis (managed).** Rejected for v1 on cost; the broker doesn't need a managed SLA.
- **SWA Free + CORS (absolute API URL).** Rejected: needs a frontend code change (relative `/api/v1`
  baseURL) and re-introduces CORS; linked backend is the same-origin design already committed.
- **User-assigned identity + federated cred for CI** (instead of an app registration). Viable and needs
  no app-reg rights; the app registration was chosen for a cleaner separation of the CI principal.

file.content:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer. environment.yml's pip section
# does `-r backend/requirements-dev.txt` (which -r's requirements.txt /
# -typecheck / -tooling), so those must exist before `conda env create`. Copy
# just the requirements files (not all of backend/) so the env-create layer cache
# busts only on a pin change, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/

content.text: Apply is churning through RPs + Postgres (the slow ones). Let me add the OCI source label to the Dockerfile so future pushes link the package to the repo:

input.old_string:
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

WORKDIR /workspace
input.new_string:
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace

FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer. environment.yml's pip section
# does `-r backend/requirements-dev.txt` (which -r's requirements.txt /
# -typecheck / -tooling), so those must exist before `conda env create`. Copy
# just the requirements files (not all of backend/) so the env-create layer cache
# busts only on a pin change, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy

ENV PATH=/opt/conda/envs/dataq/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is needed to run pytest/mypy in-container).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker service overrides `command` in compose.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

Status update while the apply finishes provisioning:

**On the package linkage (your catch):** Correct — because I pushed the first image locally, `dataq-backend` is user-scoped and unlinked from the repo. I've added the `org.opencontainers.image.source` label to [backend/Dockerfile](backend/Dockerfile) (committed). Note this only takes effect on the **next push** — so once the apply is done I'll **rebuild + push `v1` with the label**, which links the package under the repo's Packages and lets the CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` push to it. The already-running ACA pull is unaffected (still public).

**Apply progress:** ~2 min in — RP registrations (Web/KeyVault/Insights/Cache) + Postgres Flexible Server are the slow ones (~5 min total). I'll be auto-notified when it finishes, then continue with: rebuild/push linked `v1` → wire GitHub secrets/vars from outputs → run migrate job → verify API/SPA → docs.

I'll pick back up automatically when the apply completes.

content.text: Now that we are going to cloud, is plain python+pip better than conda?

Good question to raise now — the 2.84 GB image, the painful amd64 emulated build, and the cross-internet GHCR pull latency (ADR 0023) are all symptoms that point at conda. Let me ground my answer in how DataQ actually manages deps before I opine:

(non-pip lines in environment.yml) ==="; echo "(inspect above: anything under dependencies: that is NOT under pip:)"

content.content:
=== environment.yml ===
name: dataq
channels:
  - conda-forge
  - defaults
dependencies:
  - python=3.13
  - pip

  - pip:
    # Everything (runtime + typed subset + tooling + tests) flows from the
    # backend/requirements*.txt chain — the single source of truth. No package
    # versions are pinned in this file; bump them in backend/requirements*.txt.
    - -r backend/requirements-dev.txt

=== requirements.txt (first 40) ===
# DataQ backend — runtime dependencies (SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH).
#
# Everything else points here: `environment.yml` (via requirements-dev.txt), the
# CI test + audit jobs, and the production Docker image. Bump a runtime version
# HERE only.
#
# Two pip files reference these versions and stay aligned automatically:
#   - requirements-dev.txt        — `-r requirements.txt` + the test toolchain.
#   - requirements-typecheck.txt  — the minimal *typed* subset mypy needs (kept
#                                   small so the type-check job stays fast and
#                                   doesn't surface errors from heavy untyped
#                                   deps). Dependabot updates both pip files, and
#                                   the typecheck-deps-sync CI check guards the
#                                   pre-commit mypy hook against this subset.

# ── Web framework ──
fastapi==0.138.1
uvicorn[standard]==0.49.0
python-multipart==0.0.32          # required for FastAPI form data; CVE-2026-24486/40347/42561 (was 0.0.20)

# ── Task queue ──
celery[redis]==5.6.3
redis==5.2.1
croniter==6.2.2                   # cron-expression parsing for suite run schedules (A7)

# ── Database ──
sqlalchemy==2.0.51
alembic==1.18.4
psycopg2-binary==2.9.12           # swap for psycopg2 in the production image

# ── Config / validation ──
pydantic==2.13.4
pydantic-settings==2.14.2

# ── DQ engine (PINNED — GX v1 API drifts across point releases) ──
great-expectations==1.17.2

# ── Observability ──
structlog==26.1.0
opencensus-ext-azure==1.1.15      # App Insights via OpenCensus bridge
=== conda-only deps? (non-pip lines in environment.yml) ===

content.text:
This is well worth doing, and the evidence is decisive. Look at what `environment.yml` actually installs:

```yaml
dependencies:
  - python=3.13
  - pip
  - pip:
    - -r backend/requirements-dev.txt   # ← everything
```

**Conda installs nothing from conda channels except the Python interpreter and pip itself.** All ~30 runtime deps come through pip wheels already. So conda's one real advantage — prebuilt binary packages from conda-forge — is **completely unused** here. You're paying conda's cost for none of its benefit.

## Verdict: yes, `python:3.13-slim` + pip is better for the cloud target

**Concrete wins — and they're exactly the pain we just hit:**
- **Image size:** `miniconda3` base (~600 MB) + the env → your **2.84 GB** image. `python:3.13-slim` + these wheels (multi-stage) → roughly **0.8–1.2 GB**. Smaller image = faster ACA cold-start/scale-out and less cross-internet GHCR pull latency — the precise concern ADR 0023 flagged.
- **Build:** simpler and much faster under the amd64 emulation we just fought; a multi-stage build drops the compiler/toolchain from the final layer.
- **No compilation risk:** every heavy dep (pyarrow, pandas, `snowflake-connector-python`, `databricks-sql-connector`, `great-expectations`, `psycopg2-binary`) ships manylinux wheels. (requirements.txt even already notes "swap psycopg2-binary for psycopg2 in the production image.")

## Two scopes — I'd recommend the first now

1. **Slim the production image only (low risk, big win):** convert `backend/Dockerfile` to multi-stage `python:3.13-slim` + `pip install -r requirements.txt`; keep conda for local dev. Image shrinks immediately, dev workflow untouched. Minor cost: a dev(conda)/prod(pip) split.
2. **Full switch to venv+pip everywhere:** retire `environment.yml`, update `setup.sh`/CI/docs, drop the "conda only" rule. Cleaner long-term, bigger blast radius.

## The catch

"Conda only" is a **locked Week-1 decision** (CLAUDE.md §6/§11). Flipping it properly needs an **ADR superseding that lock** (exactly like ADR 0017 superseded the Python-3.11 lock) plus updates to CONTRIBUTING/CLAUDE.md — so I won't change it silently. The only reason to *keep* conda would be a future dep that's genuinely painful via pip (rare in 2026, and not your case today).

**Nice synergy:** I already owe you a rebuild+push of `v1` (to apply the OCI-source label and link the GHCR package). That rebuild could just as easily produce the **slim image** instead — folding the size win into a step we're doing anyway.

Want me to:
- **(A)** make that linkage rebuild a multi-stage slim image now (+ write the ADR), or
- **(B)** keep `v1` as-is to finish the deploy, and do the slim switch as a separate ADR'd PR afterward?

Meanwhile the apply is at ~5 min — the 4 RP registrations are the long pole; dependent resources create once they're `Registered`. I'll continue when it completes.

file.content:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer. environment.yml's pip section
# does `-r backend/requirements-dev.txt` (which -r's requirements.txt /
# -typecheck / -tooling), so those must exist before `conda env create`. Copy
# just the requirements files (not all of backend/) so the env-create layer cache
# busts only on a pin change, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy

ENV PATH=/opt/conda/envs/dataq/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is needed to run pytest/mypy in-container).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker service overrides `command` in compose.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

input.content:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Multi-stage pip build on python:3.13-slim (ADR 0025). The production image uses
# pip, NOT conda: every dependency already came through pip (environment.yml only
# `-r`'d the requirements chain — conda installed no conda-channel packages), so
# the miniconda base added ~1.5-2GB and build friction for no benefit. Conda stays
# the LOCAL-DEV tool (environment.yml / setup.sh) per CLAUDE.md; only this image
# changed. RUNTIME deps only (requirements.txt) — tests/tooling run in the dev env
# (conda locally, pip in CI), never in this image.

# ── Builder: install deps into an isolated venv ──────────────────────────────
FROM python:3.13-slim AS builder

ENV PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1

# build-essential covers any sdist-only transitive dep; it lives ONLY in this
# stage and never reaches the final image.
RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN python -m venv /opt/venv
ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH

# Copy just the requirements chain first so this layer caches on pin changes
# only, not on every code edit. requirements.txt is the runtime SINGLE SOURCE OF
# TRUTH (the dev/typecheck/tooling files are not installed into the image).
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN pip install -r backend/requirements.txt

# ── Runtime: slim base + the venv + app code ─────────────────────────────────
FROM python:3.13-slim AS runtime

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1

WORKDIR /workspace

# The fully-built venv from the builder (no compilers / apt lists in this layer).
COPY --from=builder /opt/venv /opt/venv

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is imported for runtime config).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker overrides `command` (compose / Container App),
# and the migrate job runs `alembic upgrade head`. psycopg2-binary (a wheel)
# keeps the image apt-free; the psycopg2-source swap remains a deferred nicety.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer. environment.yml's pip section
# does `-r backend/requirements-dev.txt` (which -r's requirements.txt /
# -typecheck / -tooling), so those must exist before `conda env create`. Copy
# just the requirements files (not all of backend/) so the env-create layer cache
# busts only on a pin change, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy

ENV PATH=/opt/conda/envs/dataq/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is needed to run pytest/mypy in-container).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker service overrides `command` in compose.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
